## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Delaware | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/delaware.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Delaware

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Delaware? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for DE filers.

Home › States › Delaware

Vehicle Exemption in Delaware

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Delaware's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionDelaware Amount

Vehicle exemption$15,000

Wildcard (can supplement)$0

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Delaware opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Delaware allow this - check local practice.

Delaware vehicle exemption: $15,000. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Delaware: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Delaware Filers Should Know

Delaware's $15,000 vehicle exemption is generous compared to most East Coast states.

Key facts for Delaware filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $15,000

Wildcard available: $0

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: District of Delaware

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in District of Columbia | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/district-of-columbia.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in District of Columbia

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in District of Columbia? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for DC filers.

Home › States › District of Columbia

Vehicle Exemption in District of Columbia

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether District of Columbia's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionDistrict of Columbia Amount

Vehicle exemption$2,575

Wildcard (can supplement)$850 + up to $8,075 of unused homestead

Federal exemption (if available)$4,450 per person

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in District of Columbia allow this - check local practice.

District of Columbia vehicle exemption: $2,575. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7. You can also use the federal vehicle exemption of $4,450 if that is higher.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in District of Columbia: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What District of Columbia Filers Should Know

DC's unlimited homestead exemption makes it one of the most debtor-friendly jurisdictions in the country.

Key facts for District of Columbia filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $2,575

Wildcard available: $850 + up to $8,075 of unused homestead

Federal option: Yes - $4,450 federal vehicle exemption available

Bankruptcy districts: District of Columbia

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Florida | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/florida.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Florida

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Florida? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for FL filers.

Home › States › Florida

Vehicle Exemption in Florida

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Florida's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionFlorida Amount

Vehicle exemption$1,000

Wildcard (can supplement)$4,000 (if no homestead claimed)

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Florida opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Florida allow this - check local practice.

Florida vehicle exemption: $1,000. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Florida: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Florida Filers Should Know

Florida's unlimited homestead exemption (subject to acreage limits) is one of the most powerful in the nation, but vehicle protection is very low at $1,000.

Key facts for Florida filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $1,000

Wildcard available: $4,000 (if no homestead claimed)

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: Northern District, Middle District, Southern District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Georgia | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/georgia.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Georgia

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Georgia? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for GA filers.

Home › States › Georgia

Vehicle Exemption in Georgia

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Georgia's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionGeorgia Amount

Vehicle exemption$5,000

Wildcard (can supplement)$600 + $5,000 unused homestead

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Georgia opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Georgia allow this - check local practice.

Georgia vehicle exemption: $5,000. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Georgia: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Georgia Filers Should Know

Georgia allows unused homestead exemption to be applied as a wildcard, providing flexibility for renters.

Key facts for Georgia filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $5,000

Wildcard available: $600 + $5,000 unused homestead

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: Northern District, Middle District, Southern District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Hawaii

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Hawaii? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for HI filers.

Home › States › Hawaii

Vehicle Exemption in Hawaii

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Hawaii's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionHawaii Amount

Vehicle exemption$2,575

Wildcard (can supplement)$0

Federal exemption (if available)$4,450 per person

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Hawaii allow this - check local practice.

Hawaii vehicle exemption: $2,575. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7. You can also use the federal vehicle exemption of $4,450 if that is higher.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Hawaii: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Hawaii Filers Should Know

Federal exemptions are often more favorable than Hawaii state exemptions due to Hawaii's low homestead.

Key facts for Hawaii filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $2,575

Wildcard available: $0

Federal option: Yes - $4,450 federal vehicle exemption available

Bankruptcy districts: District of Hawaii

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Idaho | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/idaho.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Idaho

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Idaho? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for ID filers.

Home › States › Idaho

Vehicle Exemption in Idaho

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Idaho's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionIdaho Amount

Vehicle exemption$10,000

Wildcard (can supplement)$800

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Idaho opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Idaho allow this - check local practice.

Idaho vehicle exemption: $10,000. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Idaho: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Idaho Filers Should Know

Idaho's $175,000 homestead and $10,000 vehicle exemptions provide strong protection for typical families.

Key facts for Idaho filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $10,000

Wildcard available: $800

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: District of Idaho

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

Bankruptcy Tools Network:

Discharge Screener ·
Research Platform ·
Exemptions by State ·

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Illinois | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/illinois.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Illinois

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Illinois? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for IL filers.

Home › States › Illinois

Vehicle Exemption in Illinois

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Illinois's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionIllinois Amount

Vehicle exemption$2,400

Wildcard (can supplement)$4,000

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Illinois opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Illinois allow this - check local practice.

Illinois vehicle exemption: $2,400. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Illinois: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Illinois Filers Should Know

Illinois has notably low homestead and vehicle exemptions compared to neighboring states. The wildcard can help bridge the gap.

Key facts for Illinois filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $2,400

Wildcard available: $4,000

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: Northern District, Central District, Southern District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Indiana | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/indiana.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Indiana

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Indiana? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for IN filers.

Home › States › Indiana

Vehicle Exemption in Indiana

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Indiana's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionIndiana Amount

Vehicle exemption$10,250 (in aggregate personal property)

Wildcard (can supplement)$0

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Indiana opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Indiana allow this - check local practice.

Indiana vehicle exemption: $10,250 (in aggregate personal property). If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Indiana: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Indiana Filers Should Know

Indiana uses an aggregate personal property exemption rather than item-by-item limits, offering some flexibility.

Key facts for Indiana filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $10,250 (in aggregate personal property)

Wildcard available: $0

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: Northern District, Southern District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Iowa | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/iowa.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Iowa

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Iowa? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for IA filers.

Home › States › Iowa

Vehicle Exemption in Iowa

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Iowa's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionIowa Amount

Vehicle exemption$7,000

Wildcard (can supplement)$0

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Iowa opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Iowa allow this - check local practice.

Iowa vehicle exemption: $7,000. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Iowa: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Iowa Filers Should Know

Iowa's unlimited homestead exemption (with acreage limits) provides powerful home protection. Vehicle exemption is moderate.

Key facts for Iowa filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $7,000

Wildcard available: $0

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: Northern District, Southern District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Kansas | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/kansas.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Kansas

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Kansas? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for KS filers.

Home › States › Kansas

Vehicle Exemption in Kansas

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Kansas's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionKansas Amount

Vehicle exemption$20,000

Wildcard (can supplement)$0

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Kansas opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Kansas allow this - check local practice.

Kansas vehicle exemption: $20,000. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Kansas: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Kansas Filers Should Know

Kansas combines an unlimited homestead with a $20,000 vehicle exemption - among the most generous vehicle protections in the U.S.

Key facts for Kansas filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $20,000

Wildcard available: $0

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: District of Kansas

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Kentucky | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/kentucky.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Kentucky

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Kentucky? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for KY filers.

Home › States › Kentucky

Vehicle Exemption in Kentucky

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Kentucky's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionKentucky Amount

Vehicle exemption$2,500

Wildcard (can supplement)$1,000

Federal exemption (if available)$4,450 per person

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Kentucky allow this - check local practice.

Kentucky vehicle exemption: $2,500. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7. You can also use the federal vehicle exemption of $4,450 if that is higher.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Kentucky: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Kentucky Filers Should Know

Kentucky's state exemptions are among the lowest in the nation. Most filers benefit from choosing federal exemptions instead.

Key facts for Kentucky filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $2,500

Wildcard available: $1,000

Federal option: Yes - $4,450 federal vehicle exemption available

Bankruptcy districts: Eastern District, Western District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

☰

Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Louisiana

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Louisiana? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for LA filers.

Home › States › Louisiana

Vehicle Exemption in Louisiana

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Louisiana's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionLouisiana Amount

Vehicle exemptionNo specific vehicle exemption

Wildcard (can supplement)$0

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Louisiana opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Louisiana allow this - check local practice.

Louisiana vehicle exemption: No specific vehicle exemption. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Louisiana: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Louisiana Filers Should Know

Louisiana is unusual - it has no specific vehicle exemption. Vehicles may be partially protected under general property rules.

Key facts for Louisiana filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: No specific vehicle exemption

Wildcard available: $0

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: Eastern District, Middle District, Western District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

Bankruptcy Tools Network:

Discharge Screener ·
Research Platform ·
Exemptions by State ·

Bankruptcy Cost ·

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Maine | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/maine.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Maine

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Maine? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for ME filers.

Home › States › Maine

Vehicle Exemption in Maine

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Maine's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionMaine Amount

Vehicle exemption$7,500

Wildcard (can supplement)$400 + $6,000 aggregate

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Maine opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Maine allow this - check local practice.

Maine vehicle exemption: $7,500. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Maine: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Maine Filers Should Know

Maine provides enhanced exemptions for elderly and disabled filers, doubling the homestead protection.

Key facts for Maine filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $7,500

Wildcard available: $400 + $6,000 aggregate

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: District of Maine

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Maryland | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/maryland.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Maryland

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Maryland? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for MD filers.

Home › States › Maryland

Vehicle Exemption in Maryland

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Maryland's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionMaryland Amount

Vehicle exemption$5,000

Wildcard (can supplement)$6,000

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Maryland opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Maryland allow this - check local practice.

Maryland vehicle exemption: $5,000. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Maryland: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Maryland Filers Should Know

Maryland's $6,000 wildcard exemption provides useful flexibility for protecting miscellaneous assets.

Key facts for Maryland filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $5,000

Wildcard available: $6,000

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: District of Maryland

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Massachusetts | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/massachusetts.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Massachusetts

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Massachusetts? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for MA filers.

Home › States › Massachusetts

Vehicle Exemption in Massachusetts

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Massachusetts's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionMassachusetts Amount

Vehicle exemption$7,500

Wildcard (can supplement)$0

Federal exemption (if available)$4,450 per person

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Massachusetts allow this - check local practice.

Massachusetts vehicle exemption: $7,500. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7. You can also use the federal vehicle exemption of $4,450 if that is higher.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Massachusetts: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Massachusetts Filers Should Know

Massachusetts has a strong $500,000 homestead, but you must file a declaration at the registry of deeds before filing bankruptcy for the full amount.

Key facts for Massachusetts filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $7,500

Wildcard available: $0

Federal option: Yes - $4,450 federal vehicle exemption available

Bankruptcy districts: District of Massachusetts

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Michigan | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/michigan.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Michigan

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Michigan? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for MI filers.

Home › States › Michigan

Vehicle Exemption in Michigan

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Michigan's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionMichigan Amount

Vehicle exemption$3,675

Wildcard (can supplement)$1,225 + $12,250 of unused homestead

Federal exemption (if available)$4,450 per person

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Michigan allow this - check local practice.

Michigan vehicle exemption: $3,675. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7. You can also use the federal vehicle exemption of $4,450 if that is higher.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Michigan: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Michigan Filers Should Know

Michigan's option to apply unused homestead as wildcard makes it flexible, especially for renters who can redirect large amounts to other property.

Key facts for Michigan filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $3,675

Wildcard available: $1,225 + $12,250 of unused homestead

Federal option: Yes - $4,450 federal vehicle exemption available

Bankruptcy districts: Eastern District, Western District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Minnesota | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/minnesota.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Minnesota

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Minnesota? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for MN filers.

Home › States › Minnesota

Vehicle Exemption in Minnesota

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Minnesota's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionMinnesota Amount

Vehicle exemption$5,000

Wildcard (can supplement)$0

Federal exemption (if available)$4,450 per person

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Minnesota allow this - check local practice.

Minnesota vehicle exemption: $5,000. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7. You can also use the federal vehicle exemption of $4,450 if that is higher.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Minnesota: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Minnesota Filers Should Know

Minnesota's $450,000 homestead is among the highest fixed-amount exemptions in the country. Agricultural property gets even more.

Key facts for Minnesota filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $5,000

Wildcard available: $0

Federal option: Yes - $4,450 federal vehicle exemption available

Bankruptcy districts: District of Minnesota

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.
