## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Mississippi | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/mississippi.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Mississippi

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Mississippi? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for MS filers.

Home › States › Mississippi

Vehicle Exemption in Mississippi

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Mississippi's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionMississippi Amount

Vehicle exemption$10,000

Wildcard (can supplement)$10,000

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Mississippi opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Mississippi allow this - check local practice.

Mississippi vehicle exemption: $10,000. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Mississippi: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Mississippi Filers Should Know

Mississippi's $10,000 wildcard and $10,000 vehicle exemptions are surprisingly generous given its low cost of living.

Key facts for Mississippi filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $10,000

Wildcard available: $10,000

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: Northern District, Southern District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

File Without a Lawyer ·
Rebuild Credit ·
Buy a House After

Free bankruptcy education. Not legal advice.

By State

This site provides general information only and does not constitute legal advice. No attorney-client relationship is created by using this site. Consult a licensed attorney for advice on your specific situation.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Missouri | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/missouri.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Missouri

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Missouri? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for MO filers.

Home › States › Missouri

Vehicle Exemption in Missouri

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Missouri's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionMissouri Amount

Vehicle exemption$3,000

Wildcard (can supplement)$600 + $1,250 head of household

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Missouri opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Missouri allow this - check local practice.

Missouri vehicle exemption: $3,000. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Missouri: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Missouri Filers Should Know

Missouri's exemptions are among the lowest in the Midwest. Strategic timing and asset structuring are often necessary to protect property.

Key facts for Missouri filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $3,000

Wildcard available: $600 + $1,250 head of household

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: Eastern District, Western District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

Bankruptcy Tools Network:

Discharge Screener ·
Research Platform ·
Exemptions by State ·
Keep Your House ·
Bankruptcy Cost ·

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Montana | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/montana.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Montana

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Montana? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for MT filers.

Home › States › Montana

Vehicle Exemption in Montana

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Montana's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionMontana Amount

Vehicle exemption$2,500

Wildcard (can supplement)$0

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Montana opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Montana allow this - check local practice.

Montana vehicle exemption: $2,500. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Montana: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Montana Filers Should Know

Montana's homestead exemption jumped to $350,000 in recent years, but vehicle protection remains low.

Key facts for Montana filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $2,500

Wildcard available: $0

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: District of Montana

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Nebraska | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/nebraska.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Nebraska

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Nebraska? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for NE filers.

Home › States › Nebraska

Vehicle Exemption in Nebraska

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Nebraska's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionNebraska Amount

Vehicle exemption$2,400

Wildcard (can supplement)$2,500

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Nebraska opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Nebraska allow this - check local practice.

Nebraska vehicle exemption: $2,400. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Nebraska: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Nebraska Filers Should Know

Nebraska provides moderate exemption levels across the board with a useful $2,500 wildcard.

Key facts for Nebraska filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $2,400

Wildcard available: $2,500

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: District of Nebraska

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Nevada | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/nevada.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Nevada

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Nevada? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for NV filers.

Home › States › Nevada

Vehicle Exemption in Nevada

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Nevada's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionNevada Amount

Vehicle exemption$15,000

Wildcard (can supplement)$1,000

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Nevada opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Nevada allow this - check local practice.

Nevada vehicle exemption: $15,000. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Nevada: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Nevada Filers Should Know

Nevada's $605,000 homestead and $15,000 vehicle exemptions are among the most generous in the western U.S.

Key facts for Nevada filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $15,000

Wildcard available: $1,000

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: District of Nevada

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in New Hampshire | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/new-hampshire.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in New Hampshire

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in New Hampshire? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for NH filers.

Home › States › New Hampshire

Vehicle Exemption in New Hampshire

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether New Hampshire's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionNew Hampshire Amount

Vehicle exemption$4,000

Wildcard (can supplement)$1,000 + $7,000 unused homestead

Federal exemption (if available)$4,450 per person

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in New Hampshire allow this - check local practice.

New Hampshire vehicle exemption: $4,000. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7. You can also use the federal vehicle exemption of $4,450 if that is higher.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in New Hampshire: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What New Hampshire Filers Should Know

Federal exemptions may be more favorable for New Hampshire renters since state exemptions tie significant value to the homestead.

Key facts for New Hampshire filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $4,000

Wildcard available: $1,000 + $7,000 unused homestead

Federal option: Yes - $4,450 federal vehicle exemption available

Bankruptcy districts: District of New Hampshire

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in New Jersey | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/new-jersey.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in New Jersey

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in New Jersey? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for NJ filers.

Home › States › New Jersey

Vehicle Exemption in New Jersey

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether New Jersey's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionNew Jersey Amount

Vehicle exemption$0 (no vehicle exemption)

Wildcard (can supplement)$1,000

Federal exemption (if available)$4,450 per person

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in New Jersey allow this - check local practice.

New Jersey vehicle exemption: $0 (no vehicle exemption). If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7. You can also use the federal vehicle exemption of $4,450 if that is higher.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in New Jersey: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What New Jersey Filers Should Know

New Jersey has no homestead or vehicle exemption under state law, making the federal exemption option essential for most filers.

Key facts for New Jersey filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $0 (no vehicle exemption)

Wildcard available: $1,000

Federal option: Yes - $4,450 federal vehicle exemption available

Bankruptcy districts: District of New Jersey

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in New Mexico | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/new-mexico.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in New Mexico

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in New Mexico? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for NM filers.

Home › States › New Mexico

Vehicle Exemption in New Mexico

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether New Mexico's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionNew Mexico Amount

Vehicle exemption$4,000

Wildcard (can supplement)$500

Federal exemption (if available)$4,450 per person

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in New Mexico allow this - check local practice.

New Mexico vehicle exemption: $4,000. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7. You can also use the federal vehicle exemption of $4,450 if that is higher.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in New Mexico: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What New Mexico Filers Should Know

Having the federal exemption option gives New Mexico filers flexibility, especially those with significant personal property.

Key facts for New Mexico filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $4,000

Wildcard available: $500

Federal option: Yes - $4,450 federal vehicle exemption available

Bankruptcy districts: District of New Mexico

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

Bankruptcy Tools Network:

Research Platform ·
Exemptions by State ·
Keep Your House ·
Bankruptcy Cost ·

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in New York | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/new-york.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in New York

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in New York? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for NY filers.

Home › States › New York

Vehicle Exemption in New York

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether New York's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionNew York Amount

Vehicle exemption$4,825

Wildcard (can supplement)$1,175 + $13,025 unused homestead

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - New York opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in New York allow this - check local practice.

New York vehicle exemption: $4,825. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in New York: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What New York Filers Should Know

New York's homestead varies dramatically by county. NYC-area counties get the highest amounts. Unused homestead can be redirected as wildcard.

Key facts for New York filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $4,825

Wildcard available: $1,175 + $13,025 unused homestead

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: Southern District, Eastern District, Northern District, Western District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in North Carolina | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/north-carolina.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in North Carolina

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in North Carolina? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for NC filers.

Home › States › North Carolina

Vehicle Exemption in North Carolina

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether North Carolina's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionNorth Carolina Amount

Vehicle exemption$3,500

Wildcard (can supplement)$5,000 + $500/dependent

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - North Carolina opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in North Carolina allow this - check local practice.

North Carolina vehicle exemption: $3,500. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in North Carolina: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What North Carolina Filers Should Know

North Carolina's wildcard includes a per-dependent bonus, making it more favorable for families.

Key facts for North Carolina filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $3,500

Wildcard available: $5,000 + $500/dependent

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: Eastern District, Middle District, Western District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in North Dakota | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/north-dakota.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in North Dakota

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in North Dakota? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for ND filers.

Home › States › North Dakota

Vehicle Exemption in North Dakota

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether North Dakota's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionNorth Dakota Amount

Vehicle exemption$1,200 per vehicle (mobile home: $100,000)

Wildcard (can supplement)$7,500

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - North Dakota opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in North Dakota allow this - check local practice.

North Dakota vehicle exemption: $1,200 per vehicle (mobile home: $100,000). If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in North Dakota: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What North Dakota Filers Should Know

North Dakota has a surprisingly strong homestead exemption at $150,000 but very low vehicle protection at $1,200.

Key facts for North Dakota filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $1,200 per vehicle (mobile home: $100,000)

Wildcard available: $7,500

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: District of North Dakota

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Ohio | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/ohio.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Ohio

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Ohio? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for OH filers.

Home › States › Ohio

Vehicle Exemption in Ohio

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Ohio's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionOhio Amount

Vehicle exemption$4,450

Wildcard (can supplement)$1,475

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Ohio opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Ohio allow this - check local practice.

Ohio vehicle exemption: $4,450. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Ohio: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Ohio Filers Should Know

Ohio's homestead exemption increased significantly in recent years, now protecting $145,425 in home equity.

Key facts for Ohio filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $4,450

Wildcard available: $1,475

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: Northern District, Southern District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Oklahoma | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/oklahoma.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Oklahoma

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Oklahoma? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for OK filers.

Home › States › Oklahoma

Vehicle Exemption in Oklahoma

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Oklahoma's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionOklahoma Amount

Vehicle exemption$7,500

Wildcard (can supplement)$0

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Oklahoma opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Oklahoma allow this - check local practice.

Oklahoma vehicle exemption: $7,500. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Oklahoma: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Oklahoma Filers Should Know

Oklahoma joins Texas, Florida, Iowa, and Kansas with an unlimited homestead (subject to acreage limits).

Key facts for Oklahoma filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $7,500

Wildcard available: $0

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: Northern District, Eastern District, Western District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Oregon | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/oregon.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Oregon

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Oregon? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for OR filers.

Home › States › Oregon

Vehicle Exemption in Oregon

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Oregon's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionOregon Amount

Vehicle exemption$3,000

Wildcard (can supplement)$400

Federal exemption (if available)$4,450 per person

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Oregon allow this - check local practice.

Oregon vehicle exemption: $3,000. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7. You can also use the federal vehicle exemption of $4,450 if that is higher.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Oregon: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Oregon Filers Should Know

Oregon's state homestead exemption is relatively low, so federal exemptions may be better for renters or those with limited equity.

Key facts for Oregon filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $3,000

Wildcard available: $400

Federal option: Yes - $4,450 federal vehicle exemption available

Bankruptcy districts: District of Oregon

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

Research Platform ·
Exemptions by State ·

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Pennsylvania | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/pennsylvania.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Pennsylvania

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Pennsylvania? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for PA filers.

Home › States › Pennsylvania

Vehicle Exemption in Pennsylvania

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Pennsylvania's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionPennsylvania Amount

Vehicle exemption$0 (no vehicle exemption)

Wildcard (can supplement)$300 in any property

Federal exemption (if available)$4,450 per person

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Pennsylvania allow this - check local practice.

Pennsylvania vehicle exemption: $0 (no vehicle exemption). If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7. You can also use the federal vehicle exemption of $4,450 if that is higher.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Pennsylvania: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Pennsylvania Filers Should Know

Like New Jersey, Pennsylvania has essentially no state homestead or vehicle exemption. Federal exemptions are virtually mandatory for property protection.

Key facts for Pennsylvania filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $0 (no vehicle exemption)

Wildcard available: $300 in any property

Federal option: Yes - $4,450 federal vehicle exemption available

Bankruptcy districts: Eastern District, Middle District, Western District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Rhode Island | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/rhode-island.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Rhode Island? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for RI filers.

Home › States › Rhode Island

Vehicle Exemption in Rhode Island

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Rhode Island's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionRhode Island Amount

Vehicle exemption$12,000

Wildcard (can supplement)$0

Federal exemption (if available)$4,450 per person

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Rhode Island allow this - check local practice.

Rhode Island vehicle exemption: $12,000. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7. You can also use the federal vehicle exemption of $4,450 if that is higher.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Rhode Island: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Rhode Island Filers Should Know

Rhode Island's $500,000 homestead and $12,000 vehicle exemptions are among the strongest in New England.

Key facts for Rhode Island filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $12,000

Wildcard available: $0

Federal option: Yes - $4,450 federal vehicle exemption available

Bankruptcy districts: District of Rhode Island

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in South Carolina | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/south-carolina.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in South Carolina

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in South Carolina? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for SC filers.

Home › States › South Carolina

Vehicle Exemption in South Carolina

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether South Carolina's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionSouth Carolina Amount

Vehicle exemption$5,600

Wildcard (can supplement)$5,600

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - South Carolina opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in South Carolina allow this - check local practice.

South Carolina vehicle exemption: $5,600. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in South Carolina: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What South Carolina Filers Should Know

South Carolina provides decent exemption levels with consistent $5,600 amounts for vehicle and wildcard.

Key facts for South Carolina filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $5,600

Wildcard available: $5,600

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: District of South Carolina

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.
