## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in South Dakota | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/south-dakota.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in South Dakota

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in South Dakota? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for SD filers.

Home › States › South Dakota

Vehicle Exemption in South Dakota

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether South Dakota's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionSouth Dakota Amount

Vehicle exemptionNo specific limit; falls under general personal property

Wildcard (can supplement)$6,000

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - South Dakota opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in South Dakota allow this - check local practice.

South Dakota vehicle exemption: No specific limit; falls under general personal property. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in South Dakota: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What South Dakota Filers Should Know

South Dakota's unlimited homestead (with acreage caps) and $6,000 wildcard provide strong protection.

Key facts for South Dakota filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: No specific limit; falls under general personal property

Wildcard available: $6,000

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: District of South Dakota

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Tennessee | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/tennessee.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Tennessee

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Tennessee? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for TN filers.

Home › States › Tennessee

Vehicle Exemption in Tennessee

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Tennessee's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionTennessee Amount

Vehicle exemptionNo specific vehicle exemption

Wildcard (can supplement)$10,000

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Tennessee opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Tennessee allow this - check local practice.

Tennessee vehicle exemption: No specific vehicle exemption. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Tennessee: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Tennessee Filers Should Know

Tennessee's $10,000 wildcard is its best feature. The homestead is very low unless you are age 62 or older.

Key facts for Tennessee filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: No specific vehicle exemption

Wildcard available: $10,000

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: Eastern District, Middle District, Western District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Texas | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/texas.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Texas

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Texas? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for TX filers.

Home › States › Texas

Vehicle Exemption in Texas

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Texas's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionTexas Amount

Vehicle exemption$30,000 per household member (part of personal property)

Wildcard (can supplement)$50,000 single / $100,000 family (personal property aggregate)

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Texas opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Texas allow this - check local practice.

Texas vehicle exemption: $30,000 per household member (part of personal property). If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Texas: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Texas Filers Should Know

Texas has unlimited homestead protection, $30,000 per person in vehicle equity, and a $50,000-$100,000 personal property aggregate. It is one of the most debtor-friendly states in the country.

Key facts for Texas filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $30,000 per household member (part of personal property)

Wildcard available: $50,000 single / $100,000 family (personal property aggregate)

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: Northern District, Eastern District, Southern District, Western District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

Bankruptcy Tools Network:

Discharge Screener ·
Research Platform ·
Exemptions by State ·
Keep Your House ·
Bankruptcy Cost ·

Chapter 7 Car ·
Chapter 13 Car ·

By State

A project of the Open Bankruptcy Project, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit (determination pending).

---

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Utah | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/utah.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Utah

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Utah? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for UT filers.

Home › States › Utah

Vehicle Exemption in Utah

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Utah's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionUtah Amount

Vehicle exemption$3,000

Wildcard (can supplement)$0

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Utah opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Utah allow this - check local practice.

Utah vehicle exemption: $3,000. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Utah: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Utah Filers Should Know

Utah's exemptions are moderate. No wildcard means every asset must fit within a specific exemption category.

Key facts for Utah filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $3,000

Wildcard available: $0

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: District of Utah

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Vermont | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/vermont.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Vermont

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Vermont? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for VT filers.

Home › States › Vermont

Vehicle Exemption in Vermont

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Vermont's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionVermont Amount

Vehicle exemption$2,500

Wildcard (can supplement)$400 + $7,000 unused homestead

Federal exemption (if available)$4,450 per person

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Vermont allow this - check local practice.

Vermont vehicle exemption: $2,500. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7. You can also use the federal vehicle exemption of $4,450 if that is higher.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Vermont: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Vermont Filers Should Know

Vermont's option to redirect unused homestead as wildcard benefits renters. Federal exemptions may be better for those without real estate.

Key facts for Vermont filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $2,500

Wildcard available: $400 + $7,000 unused homestead

Federal option: Yes - $4,450 federal vehicle exemption available

Bankruptcy districts: District of Vermont

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Virginia | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/virginia.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Virginia

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Virginia? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for VA filers.

Home › States › Virginia

Vehicle Exemption in Virginia

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Virginia's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionVirginia Amount

Vehicle exemption$6,000

Wildcard (can supplement)$5,000 + $500/dependent

Federal exemption (if available)$4,450 per person

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Virginia allow this - check local practice.

Virginia vehicle exemption: $6,000. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7. You can also use the federal vehicle exemption of $4,450 if that is higher.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Virginia: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Virginia Filers Should Know

Virginia's per-dependent wildcard bonus helps families. The federal choice gives additional flexibility depending on your asset profile.

Key facts for Virginia filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $6,000

Wildcard available: $5,000 + $500/dependent

Federal option: Yes - $4,450 federal vehicle exemption available

Bankruptcy districts: Eastern District, Western District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/washington.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Washington

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Washington? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for WA filers.

Home › States › Washington

Vehicle Exemption in Washington

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Washington's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionWashington Amount

Vehicle exemption$3,250

Wildcard (can supplement)$3,000 (includes unused personal property amounts)

Federal exemption (if available)$4,450 per person

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Washington allow this - check local practice.

Washington vehicle exemption: $3,250. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7. You can also use the federal vehicle exemption of $4,450 if that is higher.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Washington: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Washington Filers Should Know

Washington's exemption amounts are moderate for a high cost-of-living state. Federal exemptions may be preferable for some filers.

Key facts for Washington filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $3,250

Wildcard available: $3,000 (includes unused personal property amounts)

Federal option: Yes - $4,450 federal vehicle exemption available

Bankruptcy districts: Eastern District, Western District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in West Virginia | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/west-virginia.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in West Virginia

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in West Virginia? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for WV filers.

Home › States › West Virginia

Vehicle Exemption in West Virginia

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether West Virginia's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionWest Virginia Amount

Vehicle exemption$2,400

Wildcard (can supplement)$800 + $4,000 unused homestead

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - West Virginia opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in West Virginia allow this - check local practice.

West Virginia vehicle exemption: $2,400. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in West Virginia: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What West Virginia Filers Should Know

West Virginia's unused homestead can be redirected as wildcard, giving renters some additional flexibility.

Key facts for West Virginia filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $2,400

Wildcard available: $800 + $4,000 unused homestead

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: Northern District, Southern District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Wisconsin

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Wisconsin? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for WI filers.

Home › States › Wisconsin

Vehicle Exemption in Wisconsin

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Wisconsin's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionWisconsin Amount

Vehicle exemption$4,000

Wildcard (can supplement)$0

Federal exemption (if available)$4,450 per person

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Wisconsin allow this - check local practice.

Wisconsin vehicle exemption: $4,000. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7. You can also use the federal vehicle exemption of $4,450 if that is higher.

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Stay violations in Wisconsin: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Wisconsin Filers Should Know

Wisconsin's federal exemption option gives flexibility. Renters often benefit from federal wildcard rather than state homestead they cannot use.

Key facts for Wisconsin filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $4,000

Wildcard available: $0

Federal option: Yes - $4,450 federal vehicle exemption available

Bankruptcy districts: Eastern District, Western District

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Wyoming | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/states/wyoming.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7 Car
Chapter 13 Car
Car Exemptions
Reaffirmation
FAQ
By State

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Wyoming

Can you keep your car in bankruptcy in Wyoming? Vehicle exemption amounts, reaffirmation rules, cramdown options, and repossession protection for WY filers.

Home › States › Wyoming

Vehicle Exemption in Wyoming

If you are worried about losing your car in bankruptcy, the first question is how much equity you have - and whether Wyoming's vehicle exemption covers it.

ProtectionWyoming Amount

Vehicle exemption$5,000

Wildcard (can supplement)$0

Federal exemption (if available)Not available - Wyoming opted out

How equity works: If your car is worth $15,000 and you owe $12,000 on the loan, your equity is $3,000. If the exemption covers $3,000+, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7.

If your car is underwater (you owe more than it is worth), the trustee has no interest in it - your exemption covers it completely.

Chapter 7: Keeping Your Car

In Chapter 7, you must file a Statement of Intention (Form 108) within 30 days declaring what you want to do with each secured debt, including your car loan. Your options are:

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car stays with you, but the debt survives the bankruptcy. You remain personally liable if you later default.

Redemption: Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. Section 722. This works if you owe more than the car is worth and can come up with the cash (some companies specialize in redemption loans).

Surrender: Give the car back. The remaining deficiency balance is discharged.

Retain and pay (ride-through): In some districts, you may be able to keep paying without reaffirming. Not all courts in Wyoming allow this - check local practice.

Wyoming vehicle exemption: $5,000. If your equity is within this amount, the trustee cannot take your car in Chapter 7.

Chapter 13: Catching Up on Car Payments

Chapter 13 is often the better choice if you are behind on car payments. Under Chapter 13:

You keep the car while you catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option: If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. Section 506(a). The remaining balance becomes unsecured debt paid at pennies on the dollar.

Interest rate reduction: The court may set a lower interest rate on the car loan (typically prime + 1-3%) under the Till formula.

Automatic stay protection: The moment you file, the lender must stop all repossession efforts under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Cramdown savings example: If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $12,000 and qualify for cramdown, you pay $12,000 as secured debt through your plan. The $6,000 difference becomes unsecured - potentially saving thousands.

Repossession and the Automatic Stay

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. Section 362. This immediately halts all collection activity, including repossession.

If your car has not been repossessed yet: Filing stops the repo. The lender must cease all attempts to take the vehicle. If a repo agent comes after you file, they are violating a federal court order.

If your car was already repossessed: If the lender has not yet sold the car, you may be able to get it back by filing bankruptcy and invoking the automatic stay. Once the car is sold, recovery becomes much harder.

Stay violations in Wyoming: If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you can file a motion for sanctions under Section 362(k). The court may award actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

What Wyoming Filers Should Know

Wyoming's exemptions are moderate. The state has a relatively low filing rate and limited attorney availability in rural areas.

Key facts for Wyoming filers trying to keep their car:

Vehicle exemption: $5,000

Wildcard available: $0

Federal option: No - must use state exemptions

Bankruptcy districts: District of Wyoming

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. The key is understanding your equity, choosing the right chapter, and using exemptions strategically.

## Surrendering Your Car in Bankruptcy - Options and Consequences | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com - Keep My Car
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/surrender-options.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Reaffirmation
Repossession
FAQ

Options for Surrendering Your Vehicle

Sometimes giving back the car is the smartest financial decision. Here is how surrender works in bankruptcy and what it means for your debt.

Home›Surrendering Your Car in Bankruptcy - Options and Consequences | keepmycarinbankruptcy.com

When surrender makes sense

Not every car is worth keeping. If any of these apply to you, surrendering the vehicle in bankruptcy may be the better option:

You are deeply underwater - you owe $18,000 on a car worth $8,000. Even with cramdown, you would still owe the full value. If you do not need this particular car, surrender eliminates the entire debt.

The car is unreliable - ongoing repairs make it more expensive to keep than to replace.

You have a second vehicle - if one car meets your needs, surrendering the second eliminates a payment.

The interest rate is crushing - subprime auto loans at 20%+ can trap you in a cycle of negative equity.

You cannot afford the payment post-bankruptcy - your budget simply does not support the monthly cost.

How surrender works in Chapter 7

In Chapter 7, surrender is straightforward:

You indicate "surrender" on your Statement of Intention under 11 U.S.C. § 521(a)(2).

You make the car available for the lender to pick up. Some lenders arrange a time; others send a tow truck.

The lender sells the car at auction or through a dealer.

Any deficiency balance - the gap between what you owed and what the car sold for - is treated as unsecured debt and discharged in your bankruptcy.

This is the critical advantage over voluntary repossession outside of bankruptcy. Without bankruptcy, the lender can sue you for the deficiency. In bankruptcy, the deficiency dies with the discharge.

How surrender works in Chapter 13

In Chapter 13, you can surrender the car through your repayment plan. The process is:

Your plan proposes to surrender the vehicle to the lender.

The lender's secured claim becomes $0 (they get the car back).

Any deficiency becomes an unsecured claim, paid through the plan at whatever percentage your plan pays unsecured creditors - often 10-25 cents on the dollar.

This can be strategic. If you need to keep a different car but want to get rid of an expensive second vehicle, Chapter 13 surrender lets you do both.

Surrender vs. voluntary repossession

Surrender in bankruptcy and voluntary repossession outside bankruptcy look similar - you give back the car. But the financial consequences are very different:

In bankruptcy: the deficiency balance is discharged (Chapter 7) or treated as unsecured debt (Chapter 13). The lender cannot collect it.

Outside bankruptcy: the lender sells the car, adds repossession and sale costs to the balance, and sues you for the full deficiency. This can lead to wage garnishment and bank levies.

If you are considering voluntary repossession, talk to a bankruptcy attorney first. Filing bankruptcy before or after the repossession can eliminate the deficiency entirely.

Getting a replacement vehicle

After surrender, you still need to get around. Here are realistic options:

Buy a cash car - a reliable used car for $3,000-$5,000 avoids new debt entirely. This is the safest post-bankruptcy move.

Post-discharge auto loans - some lenders specialize in loans to recent bankruptcy filers. Expect higher interest rates (10-20%), but they are available. Shop credit unions first - they often offer the best terms.

In Chapter 13 - you may need court permission to take on new debt during your plan. File a motion showing the car is necessary and the terms are reasonable.

Lease - some dealers lease to people with recent bankruptcy, especially if you have steady income.

Timing matters. If you are surrendering a car in Chapter 7, arrange the pickup before your discharge date. If the lender has not retrieved the car, you may still have insurance and maintenance obligations. In Chapter 13, the plan controls the timeline.

Key takeaway: Surrendering a car in bankruptcy is not failure - it is strategy. If you are deeply underwater or the car is not worth keeping, surrender eliminates the debt cleanly. The discharge wipes out the deficiency that would follow you for years outside of bankruptcy. Sometimes the best way to protect your finances is to let go of the car and start fresh.

## Privacy Policy - keepmycarinbankruptcy.com
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/privacy.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com

Home
Privacy

Privacy Policy

How we handle your data. Short version: we don't sell it, share it, or use it for advertising.

Data We Collect

We use Google Analytics (GA4) to measure aggregate site traffic. This includes page views, session duration, approximate geographic region (country and city level), and device type. We do not collect names, email addresses, or personally identifiable information through analytics.

Aggregate event data from Google Analytics is also exported to Google BigQuery for research use. The BigQuery data is used only for internal trajectory analysis and network health reports, is not shared with third parties, and is not used for advertising.

What We Don't Do

We do not sell data. We do not run advertising. We do not collect financial information. We do not use retargeting. We do not share any user data with attorneys, law firms, creditors, debt collectors, or legal service providers.

Specifically, we do not enable Google Signals, Google Ads integration, User-ID collection, or any cross-device tracking feature in Google Analytics. GA4 operates in aggregate-traffic-measurement mode only on this site.

Cookies

We use only first-party cookies set by Google Analytics for aggregate traffic measurement. We do not use advertising cookies, tracking pixels, or third-party analytics cookies.

Third-Party Services

Google Analytics (GA4) - aggregate traffic measurement

Google BigQuery - aggregate event data warehouse for research use only; not shared, not used for advertising

Google Search Console - search query measurement (clicks, impressions, our own page rankings)

GitHub Pages - hosting

Contact Forms & Email

This site may link to contact forms or email addresses operated by the Open Bankruptcy Project. Any data you submit through those channels is handled under the Open Bankruptcy Project master privacy policy, which governs all sites in this network.

Children's Privacy

This site is not directed at children under 13. We do not knowingly collect information from children.

Data Retention

Analytics data is retained for 14 months per Google's standard retention settings. After that window, individual event data is automatically deleted by Google; aggregate report metrics remain available.

Your Rights

You may request deletion of any personal data we hold by contacting info@openbankruptcyproject.org. California residents have additional rights under CCPA. EU residents have additional rights under GDPR. All such requests are handled by the Open Bankruptcy Project.

Network Policy

This site is part of the Bankruptcy Transparency Network operated by the Open Bankruptcy Project, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit (determination pending). The same privacy standards apply across every site in the network. The authoritative master policy is maintained at https://openbankruptcyproject.org/privacy.html.

Changes

We may update this policy from time to time. Changes are reflected on the master policy first and then mirrored across all network sites. Check the master policy for the most current version.

Effective: March 30, 2026  |  Updated: April 11, 2026

Home  ·  Privacy  ·  Open Bankruptcy Project

Part of the Bankruptcy Transparency Network. Educational information only - not legal advice.

No ads. No affiliate links. No data sold. Supported by donations.

## Additional FAQ Pages


### Can I keep my car in Chapter 13? - Keep My Car in Bankruptcy
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/paa/can-i-keep-my-car-in-chapter-13.html

Skip to content

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com

Home
Questions

Home › Common Questions › Can I keep my car in Chapter 13?

People Also Ask

Can I keep my car in Chapter 13?

Yes. Chapter 13 is specifically designed to help you keep property while catching up on payments. If you are behind on car payments, you can cure the arrears through your repayment plan. If you have owned the car for more than 910 days, you may be able to reduce the loan balance to the car's current value through a cramdown.

Chapter 13 provides powerful tools for keeping your vehicle, even if you are behind on payments or owe more than it is worth.

Curing Arrears

If you are behind on car payments, Chapter 13 allows you to catch up over the life of your 3-5 year repayment plan. As long as you make your plan payments, the lender cannot repossess. This is one of the primary reasons people choose Chapter 13 over Chapter 7.

Cramdown (910 Rule)

If you purchased your car more than 910 days (approximately 2.5 years) before filing, you may be able to "cram down" the loan to the car's current fair market value. For example, if you owe $18,000 on a car worth $10,000, the court can reduce the secured claim to $10,000. The remaining $8,000 becomes unsecured debt that may be discharged.

Interest Rate Reduction

In a Chapter 13 cramdown, the court also sets a new interest rate using the "Till" formula: prime rate plus a risk adjustment of 1-3%. If your original loan was at 18% from a buy-here-pay-here lot, your cramdown rate might be 7-8%, dramatically reducing your monthly payment.

The 910-Day Limitation

If you purchased the car within 910 days of filing (the "hanging paragraph" of Section 1325(a)), you cannot cram down the loan. You must pay the full balance at the contract rate. This is why timing matters - if you are close to the 910-day mark, waiting to file can save you thousands.

Chapter 13 is often the better option for vehicle issues, especially if you have a high-interest loan, are behind on payments, or are significantly underwater on the loan.

Related Guides

Chapter 13 Car

Index

Behind On Payments

### Can the trustee take my car in Chapter 7? - Keep My Car in Bankruptcy
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/paa/can-the-trustee-take-my-car-in-chapter-7.html

Skip to content

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com

Home
Questions

Home › Common Questions › Can the trustee take my car in Chapter 7?

People Also Ask

Can the trustee take my car in Chapter 7?

The trustee can only take your car if your equity exceeds your state's motor vehicle exemption. If you owe more than the car is worth (negative equity), you have no equity at risk and the trustee has no interest in the vehicle. In most Chapter 7 cases, filers keep their vehicles.

Whether the Chapter 7 trustee can take your car depends entirely on equity and exemptions.

The Equity Calculation

Equity = Fair Market Value - Amount Owed. If you owe $15,000 on a car worth $12,000, you have negative equity (-$3,000) and the trustee has zero interest in your vehicle. If you own a car worth $8,000 free and clear, you have $8,000 in equity that must fit within your exemption.

Motor Vehicle Exemptions

Every state has a motor vehicle exemption that protects a certain amount of equity:

Federal exemption (available in some states): $4,450 per debtor

Some states offer higher amounts: Missouri ($3,000), Kansas ($20,000), California (up to $7,500)

Married couples filing jointly may be able to double the exemption (stack)

Wildcard exemptions can sometimes supplement a low vehicle exemption

What Happens If Equity Exceeds the Exemption?

If your equity exceeds the exemption, the trustee theoretically could sell the car to pay creditors. However, in practice the trustee must consider whether the sale would generate meaningful funds after paying exemptions, sales costs, and trustee fees. Many trustees will abandon interest in a vehicle where the excess equity is small.

You may also have the option to pay the trustee the non-exempt equity amount in cash to keep the vehicle, though this must be negotiated.

Related Guides

Chapter 7 Car

Car Exemptions

### Common Questions - Keep My Car in Bankruptcy
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/paa/index.html

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com

Home
Questions

Common Questions

Clear, data-backed answers to the most frequently asked questions

Can the trustee take my car in Chapter 7?

What is a reaffirmation agreement for a car loan?

Can I keep my car in Chapter 13?

How Bankruptcy Exemptions Protect Your Property

Bankruptcy exemptions are the legal mechanism that allows you to keep essential property when filing bankruptcy. Every state has its own set of exemptions, and some states also allow you to use federal bankruptcy exemptions instead. Common categories include homestead (protecting equity in your home), motor vehicle, household goods, retirement accounts (which receive nearly unlimited protection under federal law), tools of the trade, and wildcard exemptions that can be applied to any property.

Frequently Asked Questions

What are bankruptcy exemptions?

Bankruptcy exemptions are laws that protect certain property from being taken by creditors or a bankruptcy trustee. Common exemptions cover your home (homestead exemption), vehicle, household goods, retirement accounts, and tools of your trade. Each state sets its own exemption amounts, and some states allow you to choose between state and federal exemptions.

Can I keep my car in bankruptcy?

In most cases, yes. Your state's motor vehicle exemption protects equity in your car up to a certain dollar amount. If your car equity is below the exemption limit, you keep it. In Chapter 13, you can also restructure car loan payments. In Chapter 7, you may need to reaffirm the car loan or redeem the vehicle.

What is the homestead exemption?

The homestead exemption protects equity in your primary residence from creditors in bankruptcy. The amount varies significantly by state, from a few thousand dollars to unlimited protection in some states like Texas and Florida. The federal homestead exemption is approximately $27,900 per person as of 2026.

Part of the Bankruptcy Transparency Network

1328f.com
openbankruptcyproject.org

Free bankruptcy education. Not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for your specific situation.

### What is a reaffirmation agreement for a car loan? - Keep My Car in Bankruptcy
Source: https://keepmycarinbankruptcy.com/paa/what-is-a-reaffirmation-agreement-for-a-car-loan.html

Skip to content

keepmycarinbankruptcy.com

Home
Questions

Home › Common Questions › What is a reaffirmation agreement for a car loan?

People Also Ask

What is a reaffirmation agreement for a car loan?

A reaffirmation agreement is a contract you sign during Chapter 7 bankruptcy to keep your car and continue making loan payments. By reaffirming, you agree that the car loan survives your bankruptcy discharge. You remain personally liable for the debt, and the lender agrees not to repossess as long as you stay current.

Reaffirmation is the most common way to keep a financed vehicle in Chapter 7 bankruptcy.

How Reaffirmation Works

After you file Chapter 7, you file a Statement of Intention indicating what you want to do with each secured debt. For a car you want to keep, you state your intention to reaffirm. The lender then sends a reaffirmation agreement, which you sign and file with the court.

What You Are Agreeing To

By signing the reaffirmation agreement, you are agreeing that:

The car loan is not discharged in your bankruptcy

You remain personally liable for the full balance

The lender keeps its lien and its right to repossess if you default

If you later default and the car is repossessed, you could owe a deficiency balance

Court Approval

If you have an attorney, your attorney must certify that the reaffirmation does not impose an undue hardship. If you are filing pro se, the court holds a hearing to evaluate whether the reaffirmation is in your best interest. The judge may decline to approve it if the payments appear unaffordable.

Alternatives to Reaffirmation

Redemption: Pay the lender the current fair market value of the car in a lump sum (Section 722). Useful when you owe much more than the car is worth.

Ride-through: Some circuits allow you to keep the car by simply continuing payments without signing a formal agreement. Not available everywhere.

Surrender: Return the car and discharge the remaining balance.

Related Guides

Reaffirmation

Chapter 7 Car

Index
